exemplar.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #1: Do historians and human scientists have an ethical obligation to follow the directive: "do not ignore contradictory evidence"? Discuss with reference to history and the human sciences.

strengths: Maintains a sustained and coherent focus on the prescribed title, explicitly engaging the ethical obligation to consider contradictory evidence

Effectively links claims to both history and the human sciences through well-chosen, domain-specific examples (e.g., Zinn on Columbus, Cold-War historiographies, Friedmanâs monetarism, Kansas tax cuts, Greek austerity)
Presents clear, logically structured arguments and demonstrates awareness and evaluation of contrasting perspectives (traditionalist vs. revisionist historians, Keynesian vs. monetarist economists)
Incorporates epistemological criteria (coherence and consensus tests) to assess the quality of evidence
weaknesses: Depth of critical evaluation and exploration of implications is uneven; key conceptual tools (consequentialism, epistemic relativism) are introduced but only briefly applied
Transitions between sections (especially between history and human sciences examples) can be abrupt, which occasionally weakens coherence
Some theoretical claims (e.g., ethical ârankingâ of obligations, secondary status of contradictory evidence in human sciences) lack full justification or supporting examples
A few assertions (framing effects, consensus as truth) are stated without linkage to underlying theories of truth or detailed elaboration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay clearly meets the descriptors of the 7â8 band: it sustains a focused discussion of the title, links effectively to two areas of knowledge, and uses clear, coherent arguments supported by specific examples while evaluating different viewpoints. However, lapses in the depth of conceptual analysis, uneven exploration of implications, and occasional abrupt transitions prevent it from achieving the consistently insightful and sophisticated evaluation required for the top band (9â10). A score of 7 accurately reflects this quality distribution.

annotations.comment: The student effectively restates the prescribed title in the introduction, establishing clear focus on the knowledge question about the ethical obligation to consider contradictory evidence.
A clear definition of ââcontradictory evidenceââ is offered, providing a strong conceptual foundation for the later discussion of how knowers should engage with opposing information.
Transitions between the discussion of historians and human scientists are abrupt; adding clear linking sentences would improve coherence and guide the reader through each Area of Knowledge.
The introduction invokes consequentialism but does not explain why this framework is most suitable; the student should contrast it with other ethical theories to deepen the analysis.
This introductory sentence anticipates a discussion of Friedman but could be strengthened by previewing the specific epistemic point to be made.
This paragraph lists Neoliberal policy outcomes but does not critically assess their long-term societal impacts; deeper evaluation of consequences would enhance the insight.
The assertion that historians may ignore evidence once consensus exists is too categorical; the student should address situations where minority dissent yields new insights.
The distinctions between traditionalist, revisionist, and neo-revisionist views are mentioned but not clearly defined; clarifying their methodological differences would deepen the analysis.

The claim that contradictory evidence is a ââsecondary obligationââ for human scientists is asserted without substantiation; the student should provide an example or rationale to justify this hierarchy.
The acknowledgement of Zinnâs socialist bias is superficial; the student should examine how this lens shapes his selective use of evidence and its ethical implications.
The Howard Zinn example is well chosen to illustrate how revisiting marginalized perspectives employs contradictory evidence to challenge traditionalist narratives.
The transition to human sciences is abrupt; consider adding a signpost sentence that explicitly links the historical discussion to the upcoming economic example.
The explanation of the paradigm shift from Keynesianism to monetarism is clear and effectively shows how contradictory evidence can drive theoretical evolution.
The critique of Zinnâs anachronistic application of germ theory shows strong critical engagement and recognizes the importance of historical context in evaluating evidence.

The statement that ââcontradictory evidence does not become objective truthââ is asserted but not linked to any theory of truth; integrate a reference to coherence or correspondence theories to strengthen the point.
The use of historiographical perspectives on the Cuban Missile Crisis exemplifies epistemic relativism and shows a good awareness of multiple valid interpretations.
The David Irving case is relevant but would benefit from analysis of the ethical repercussions of Holocaust denial on collective memory and justice.
The omission critique of Pattersonâs narrative needs supporting citations; specify which primary or secondary sources he overlooks to validate the claim.
When discussing the Philips curve, include the formal equation (e.g., $$\\pi = \\pi^e - \\\\alpha(u - u^n)$$) to demonstrate precise conceptual understanding.
Introducing coherence and consensus tests adds valuable epistemological criteria and shows awareness of the need to evaluate evidence against established standards.
The consensus test discussion could be enriched by referencing social epistemology frameworks, such as Kuhnâs notion of paradigm communities and expert consensus.
To strengthen the conclusion, explicitly discuss the broader implications for responsible knowledge production and the role of TOK awareness in everyday reasoning.
Consider integrating insights from testimonial injustice literature to nuance the relationship between consensus and ethical engagement with counter-evidence.
The Kansas tax-cut example vividly demonstrates how ideological dogmatism can block the consideration of valid contradictory evidence in policy making.
The concept of dogmatism is invoked but not defined in detail; quoting Caldeiroâs precise definition would strengthen the theoretical basis.
The Greek austerity case effectively illustrates the role of practical constraints in limiting epistemic obligation, adding valuable context dependence to the essay.
The discussion of Varoufakisâs constraints lacks explicit linkage back to the central directive; a brief reminder of its relevance would tighten the argument.
The standalone clause ââoften context-dependantââ reads as a fragment; integrating it smoothly into the previous sentence would improve flow.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes the main arguments and reiterates the nuanced view that ethical obligations vary by context, demonstrating coherence.
Consider ending with a reflective question or illustration that connects the essayâs abstract concepts back to studentsâ personal knowledge experiences.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
